1: A type: Moab’s crime — burning the bones of the king of Edom to lime. Desecrating the dead, even an enemy’s dead, violates a fundamental human dignity that crosses all national boundaries. This offense against a corpse signals that Moab has forfeited its humanity in its hatred. Christ’s burial is attended with spices and honor even amid the shame of crucifixion (John 19:39-40); and his resurrection overcomes the ultimate dishonor of death. The divine care for the body even in judgment is implicit in Amos’s condemnation of desecration.
2: A shadow: fire on Moab, consuming the fortresses of Kerioth; Moab falls in turmoil. The collapse of Moab under judgment is part of the nations-oracle sequence in which all human imperial arrangements fall. The NT’s eschatological vision of the nations at the Last Day (Matthew 25:32: “all the nations will be gathered before him”) gives the Amos oracles their ultimate scope — not just Aram, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, and Moab, but all nations stand before the Son of Man.
3: A shadow: the ruler and all his princes are cut off. The comprehensive fall of Moab’s leadership echoes the NT’s declaration that all earthly authority is provisional and subordinate: “Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power” (1 Corinthians 15:24). Christ’s reign is the last act of this nations-oracle drama.
4: A type: Judah’s crime — rejecting the law of the LORD and not keeping his statutes, following lies. The inclusion of Judah in the oracle-sequence is the literary trap: Israel has been listening to judgment on surrounding nations with satisfaction, only to find the charges escalate to include the covenant people. This is the rhetorical move Paul executes in Romans 1–3: Gentile sin (Amos 1) → Jewish sin (Amos 2) → “all have sinned” (Amos 3:23). Christ is the one who fulfills what Judah failed to fulfill: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them” (Matthew 5:17).
5: A shadow: fire on Judah consuming the fortresses of Jerusalem. The destruction of Jerusalem’s defenses — whether by Nebuchadnezzar in 586 BCE or, in Jesus’s prediction, by Rome in 70 CE — is the outcome of covenant unfaithfulness. Christ weeps over Jerusalem: “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes” (Luke 19:41-42). Amos’s fire-on-Jerusalem and Jesus’s lament are the same prophetic grief — that the covenant city does not recognize its covenant Lord.
6: A fulfillment-context: Israel’s crime — selling the righteous for silver and the poor for a pair of sandals. This verse launches the most famous section of Amos: Israel’s social injustice as covenant violation. Christ’s identification with the poor is the direct NT counterpart: “Truly I say to you, as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me” (Matthew 25:40). The selling of the righteous for silver also echoes Judas’s betrayal of Christ for thirty pieces of silver — the price of a slave (Matthew 26:15; Zechariah 11:12-13).
7: A type: trampling the heads of the poor into the dust and pushing the afflicted off the road; father and son going to the same woman, defiling YHWH’s name. The poor are physically pushed aside to make way for the wealthy; the covenant’s sexual ethics are violated. Jesus begins his ministry announcing good news to the poor (Luke 4:18) and consistently places the crushed and the marginalized at the center of his kingdom — the reversal of exactly this pattern. The dishonor of YHWH’s name through social injustice is reversed in Christ, who hallows the Father’s name by embodying justice.
8: A type: garments taken in pledge spread out beside every altar; wine from fines drunk in the house of their God. The pledged garment was property that belonged to the poor (Exodus 22:26-27: the creditor must return a debtor’s cloak by nightfall, for it is his covering). Using it as altar-furniture while drinking wine extracted through unjust fines is a double sacrilege: exploitation plus false worship. Jesus’s cleansing of the temple (John 2:13-17) addresses the same fusion of commercial exploitation and official worship — turning the house of prayer into a den of robbers.
9: A type: YHWH destroyed the Amorite — tall as cedars, strong as oaks, destroyed root and branch. The conquest narrative is invoked to establish YHWH’s saving history as the ground on which his current charge against Israel rests. Christ brings the same rhetorical move: “You search the Scriptures because you think that in them you have eternal life; and it is they that bear witness about me” (John 5:39). Israel’s salvation history — Exodus, conquest, prophets — all bears witness to the one who is the ground and goal of that history.
10: A type: “I also brought you up from the land of Egypt, led you through the wilderness forty years, and gave you the land of the Amorite.” The Exodus-and-wilderness-and-inheritance sequence is the OT’s master-narrative of redemption. The NT reads Christ as fulfilling this pattern: he is the new Moses who leads the new Exodus (Luke 9:31: “his departure [exodon] which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem”); he passes through the wilderness forty days (Matthew 4:1-11); he gives the new inheritance (“blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth,” Matthew 5:5).
11: A type: YHWH raised up prophets from Israel’s sons and Nazirites from Israel’s young men. The prophetic and Nazirite offices were YHWH’s gifts to Israel — channels of revelation and consecration. Christ is the fulfillment of both: the Prophet greater than Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15; Acts 3:22) and, in Luke 1:35, consecrated from birth in a manner that parallels the Nazirite dedication (though not identical to the Mosaic Nazirite law). When Israel despised these gifts (Amos 2:12), they were preparing the rejection of the Prophet himself: “He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him” (John 1:11).
12: A type: “But you gave the Nazirites wine to drink and commanded the prophets, ‘Do not prophesy.’” The silencing of the prophets is the pattern of Israel’s history that Jesus explicitly invokes: “Woe to you, for you build the tombs of the prophets whom your fathers killed” (Luke 11:47). Jerusalem is the city that kills the prophets and stones those sent to her (Matthew 23:37). The command “Do not prophesy” reaches its ultimate expression in the Sanhedrin’s command to Jesus’s disciples: “that they speak no more to anyone in this name” (Acts 4:17-18).
13: A type: “Watch — I will crush you in your tracks, as a cart loaded with sheaves presses down.” The crushing weight of judgment against Israel who has crushed the poor is a form of divine lex talionis — the same crushing comes back on the crushers. Christ is the one who has borne this weight: “Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows” (Isaiah 53:4); he was crushed for our iniquities (Isaiah 53:5 mĕdukkāʾ). The judgment-weight that Amos announces lands on Christ on behalf of those who trust him.
14: A shadow: “Flight will fail the swift, the strong will find no strength, and the warrior will not escape with his life.” The comprehensive futility of escape from YHWH’s judgment is the NT’s message about the final day: “from the presence of him who is seated on the throne and from the wrath of the Lamb, for the great day of their wrath has come, and who can stand?” (Revelation 6:16-17). The inescapability of judgment that Amos announces about Israel in the Assyrian crisis becomes in Revelation the pattern of the last judgment from which no earthly power provides refuge.
15: A shadow: bowman, swift soldier, and horseman alike cannot escape. The military vocabulary exhausts the categories of human strength — all fail. The NT counterpart: “There is no creature hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account” (Hebrews 4:13). The only refuge from the divine judge is the divine judge himself, which is the paradox of the gospel: “whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life. He does not come into judgment” (John 5:24).
16: A shadow: even the most courageous warrior flees naked on that day. The stripping of the warrior — the reversal of the military hero — is the ultimate humiliation. The great reversal of the Day of the LORD runs throughout the Prophets: the mighty are brought low (Isaiah 2:12-17; Luke 1:52). Christ is the one who was himself stripped and humiliated at Golgotha (John 19:23-24) so that the stripping of judgment might not fall on those who trust him; and who, in his return, strips away the pretensions of all false strength (Revelation 19:11-16).
